html:
PT Berlian Laju Tanker Tbk, IDX:BLTA

BLTA is not the clean “unlevered tanker NAV” story the old drafts implied. The better thesis is more specific: a formerly distressed Indonesian tanker operator has rebuilt into a 10-vessel chemical and LPG platform, but the market still treats it like a special-monitoring penny stock while the income statement understates the forward cash power of the new fleet.

At the latest live quote I found, BLTA trades around IDR 47, or about IDR 1.22 trillion / USD 70 million of equity value on 25.94 billion shares. The company’s 2025 annual report says it operates 2 LPG tankers and 8 chemical tankers, with MT Hartati, Gas Lombok, and MT Hatnyanawati added to the fleet. That matters because 2025 already showed the operating inflection: revenue rose 20.8% to USD 49.1 million, TCE revenue rose 24.6% to USD 38.8 million, and operating cash flow was USD 20.0 million despite reported net profit of only USD 2.5 million.

The market’s lazy read is: “old BLTA, bankruptcy history, low price stock, weak EPS, no dividend.” The variant read is: “fixed-cost leased fleet plus niche domestic cargo exposure creates torque if 2026 vessel additions land.”

The key correction to the prior thesis is that BLTA is not debt free. At 2025 year-end it had USD 24.7 million cash, but also USD 12.1 million loans, USD 12.5 million other payables, and USD 52.3 million lease liabilities. Five vessels are owned, while several operating vessels are leased from related parties. This makes the asset thesis less clean, but the cash-flow thesis more interesting. Lease leverage means reported liabilities look heavy, but it also means incremental utilization and new cargo volumes can fall through quickly once fixed vessel costs are covered.

The operating base is better than the headline EPS suggests. BLTA’s largest disclosed customers in 2025 were PT Pertamina International Shipping at 21.6% of revenue and PT Freeport Indonesia at 19.6%. That is concentrated, but these are real industrial counterparties, not speculative spot-only cargoes. Related-party revenue was only 1.01%, which helps, although the related-party lease structure remains a governance discount.

The forward setup is explicit. Management’s 2026 plan calls for adding a 19,900 DWT chemical tanker by lease, buying a 17,000 DWT oil-chemical tanker, and adding a 3,500 CBM LPG carrier to replace an older vessel. Funding is expected from internal resources. Management also targets roughly 70% time-charter-out and 30% spot exposure, which reduces pure freight-rate downside while keeping some upside if regional rates stay firm.

The demand backdrop supports this niche better than generic global tanker headlines. Indonesia’s LPG demand is still rising, with ESDM-linked reporting pointing to 2026-2027 LPG needs near 10 million tons, up from 8.6 million tons, and imports expected to reach 83.97% of demand. BLTA’s domestic small-LPG exposure is not the same as VLGC speculation, but it benefits from the same structural fact: Indonesia needs more LPG logistics. On chemicals, the global market is not euphoric. SSY’s 2026 chemical tanker outlook calls the market fragile, with rates supported by rerouting and inefficiency rather than a clean demand boom. That is why the right multiple is not a peak shipping multiple. The upside case is about BLTA’s own fleet expansion and under-discovered cash flow, not a heroic global chemical cycle.

Valuation is now about forward EBITDA and owner cash flow. Trailing EBITDA is roughly USD 24-25 million, putting BLTA near 5x EV/EBITDA after including lease liabilities. That is not optically screaming cheap, but if the 2026 fleet plan lifts revenue toward USD 60-65 million and EBITDA toward USD 30-34 million, the multiple falls to roughly 3.5-4.0x forward EV/EBITDA. After lease and debt service, equity free cash flow could move from mid-single-digit millions to USD 8-12 million, giving a forward cash yield in the low-to-mid teens on today’s equity value.

The social/retail context reinforces the mispricing. BLTA’s Stockbit page shows special notation / special monitoring context and retail-style chatter around Q4 earnings and FCA exit hopes. This is not being priced as a maritime cash-flow compounder. It is being priced as an Indonesian penny stock with a bad chart. That stigma is the opportunity, but also the reason sizing has to be disciplined.

The bear case is real. If the related-party leases are economically unfavorable, if the new vessels absorb cash without earning acceptable returns, or if chemical/LPG cargo rates weaken, the equity can trade back to distressed book multiples. Liquidity is thin, the company has no dividend discipline, and reported net profit is still too small to force institutional discovery. The old restructuring scars are not imaginary.

The base case is that 2026 is the first year the rebuilt fleet becomes visible. Revenue grows, cash generation remains strong, cash stays above the pre-2025 level even after vessel investment, and the market begins valuing BLTA as a small operating tanker platform rather than a zombie restructuring stub. A still-discounted 5.5-6.0x forward EBITDA valuation on USD 31-32 million of EBITDA, less roughly USD 50 million of lease-adjusted net debt, supports about IDR 85-95 per share.

The bull case requires the market to notice that BLTA has become a niche Indonesia chemical/LPG logistics platform with real counterparties, a refreshed fleet, and internally funded growth. If EBITDA reaches the high-30s, net debt declines, and the stock exits the penalty box, 7x EBITDA supports a share price well above IDR 150.

At IDR 47 current price:

Case Target Return
Bear IDR 25 -47%
Base IDR 90 +91%
Bull IDR 160 +240%
